summary: Split-package dropper: XOR-encoded catbox.moe URL paired with executor dependency
details: lib/registry.js exports two byte arrays (`_ep` and `_p`) annotated as 'processed at runtime by the consumer for portability'. XOR-decoding `_ep` with the repeating key 'endpoint' yields `https://files.catbox.moe` — an anonymous, throwaway-TTL file-hosting service commonly used as a dropper staging host — and `_p` decodes to a sibling file path. The package itself contains no code that consumes these arrays; instead it declares a runtime dependency on `bytecraft@^1.5.0`, which is the intended consumer of the encoded URL and supplies the fetch-and-execute primitive. The split-package design (lure exports the obfuscated payload location, declared dependency holds the executor and key handling) is structured to defeat per-package scanning: neither half in isolation contains a complete attack. The cover-story comment is inconsistent with any legitimate validation library — there is no benign reason to XOR-encode a hostname into a byte array. On `npm install endpointmap`, npm resolves and installs `bytecraft`, which then has both the decoder logic and the encoded catbox.moe URL available to fetch and execute attacker-controlled bytes.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
